1.description: Share of French imports/exports in Le Havre. This figure shows parallel trends and illustrates the effect of central clearing on France-level trade flows. Panel A plots the share of French imports and exports arriving in Le Havre and leaving from Le Havre, respectively. The shares are plotted for imports and exports in terms of both volume (weight in 100 kg) and value (current FRF). Panel B plots the share of French coffee imports arriving in Le Havre (solid line) and the weighted average share of control commodities arriving in Le Havre (dashed line). In Panel B, the effects are net of the sample mean, to account for differences in levels between imports of coffee and of other commodities. In both panels, the dotted vertical line corresponds to the creation of the CLAM in December 1882. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

2.description: Effect of central clearing on Europe-wide trade flows. This figure plots the effect of central clearing on Europe-wide trade flows. The solid blue line is the average share of coffee imports from France across the seven sample countries, where each country is weighted by its volume of imports. The dashed purple line is the average share of imports of control commodities from France across the seven sample countries, where each country and commodity is weighted by its volume of imports. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

3.description: The 1886 to 1887 coffee price boom. This figure shows monthly coffee prices in FRF/ton over the 1878 to 1893 period. The 1886 to 1887 price boom is due to exceptionally low crops in Brazil, the largest coffee-producing country worldwide. Data are based on prices in Hamburg. Source: Tapolski (1896), converted into FRF/kg. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

4.description: Coffeeconsumption during the 1887 boom. This figure plots log-consumption of coffee, net of a fixed effect, during the 1887 coffee price boom. Coffee consumption is shown for France, Germany, Europe (excluding France and Germany), and the world (excluding Europe). The dashed vertical line corresponds to 1887, when the coffee price boom took place. Data are from Rufenacht (1955). [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

5.description: Number of commodity traders in Le Havre. This figure plots a measure of the number of commodity traders (all commodities combined) at a yearly frequency in Le Havre over the 1876 to 1886 period. Data are from yearly copies of theAlmanach du Commerce du Havreand sum the number of commodity professionals active in the harbor at the beginning of the year. I exclude a few observations for which the occupation is not directly related to commodities trading (notably, bankers and warehouses). I include observations for which the occupation is missing (about 15% of the data). The first vertical dashed line (in red) corresponds to the 1880 coffee crisis, which started in the second half of 1880. The second vertical dashed line (in black) corresponds to the creation of the CLAM in December 1882. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

6.description: Trading volume in coffee futures. This figure plots trading volume in coffee futures at a daily frequency. Volume is expressed in thousand bags (1 bag≈60 kg). The dotted vertical line corresponds to the creation of the CLAM in December 1882. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

7.description: Trader turnover. This figure plots a measure of the turnover of commodity traders (all commodities combined) at a yearly frequency in Le Havre over the 1875 to 1889 period. Data are from yearly copies of theAlmanach du Commerce du Havreand sum the number of commodity professionals active in the harbor at the beginning of the year. I exclude a few observations for which the occupation is not directly related to commodities trading (notably, bankers and warehouses). I include observations for which the occupation is missing (about 15% of the data). The vertical dashed line (in black) corresponds to the creation of the CLAM in December 1882. The horizontal dashed lines (in red) correspond to the mean turnover in the pre-CLAM and post-CLAM sample periods. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
